content.keyRisk: One file optimizes the loader and the single-source-of-truth invariant but gets awkward to hand-edit as it grows toward 12 sections; one-to-many is a safe additive split later, while many-to-one is the breaking direction.
content.summary: Keep all configuration in one hand-editable file with one namespaced [commands.<name>] section per subcommand, loaded by a single parse-merge-with-defaults pass; unknown/future sections are preserved so growing from 6 to 12 commands stays additive and never breaking.

Single-file with namespaced [commands.<name>] sections is the right default, but its load-bearing risk is merge-with-defaults semantics, not file count: once defaults live in code the one 'authoritative' file is only half the truth. Concrete failure — a replace-style merge on a list key (exclude) silently drops shipped defaults when a user adds one entry, and the only clean fix (switch to list-merge) breaks every existing user under 'no breaking changes'. Freeze the per-key merge rule at v1.

Concede the contrarian's point: the load-bearing risk is the per-key merge rule, not the file count, and it is fixable inside the single-file design. Freeze the merge contract at v1 (user-written keys win verbatim, lists replace not element-merge) and ship a materialized default config so lists are never silently half-specified. This keeps one deterministic parse-then-replace pass, keeps the file hand-editable, and stays additive from 6 to 12 commands. Accepted residual cost: forward-only upgrade drift on customized list keys.

activeDisagreements.point: Whether 'parse-merge-with-defaults' is simultaneously simple and non-breaking as defaults evolve.

positions.stance: Frozen replace-per-key merge + materialized defaults keeps it simple and non-breaking
Merge-with-defaults is the load-bearing hazard; a replace-style list merge silently drops shipped defaults and the fix breaks existing users

agreements.point: A single namespaced config file is the right default for a 6-to-12 command single-binary CLI; growth stays additive.

insights.insight: The real risk lives in the per-key merge contract, not in file count; freezing scalar-vs-list merge semantics at v1 is the decision that actually protects 'no breaking changes'.
Shipping a materialized default config closes the silent list-drop failure without introducing un-undoable list-merge logic.

openQuestions: Must new default list entries shipped in later releases reach users who customized that list? If yes, plain replace is insufficient and versioned-default migration must be decided before v1 freezes the merge contract.
Does 'preserve unknown sections' extend to unknown keys inside a known section, and to user comments and key ordering on write-back?
synthesis.recommendation: Use one hand-editable config file with a namespaced [commands.<name>] section per subcommand. The main trade-off is loader simplicity and a single source of truth (one parse, one precedence story, additive growth from 6 to 12 commands) versus the hand-editing/merge-review ergonomics of one growing file. Because one-to-many is a safe additive split later while many-to-one is the breaking direction (r1-msg-001), single-file is the lower-regret default under the 'no breaking changes' constraint.
synthesis.recommendation_summary: Single file, namespaced per command, with a frozen replace-per-key merge contract and materialized defaults.

topic: Config layout for a small CLI tool
